input:
<passage id="p0001" order="1" role="thesis" section_title="Intro" span_start="0" span_end="120">
If moral realism is true, then some normative truths hold independently of attitudes.
</passage>

<passage id="p0002" order="2" role="objection" section_title="Critique" span_start="121" span_end="260">
One might worry that independent normative facts are metaphysically queer in Mackie's sense.
</passage>

label.text: Independent normative facts might be metaphysically queer in Mackie's sense.

input:
<passage id="p0001" order="1" role="definition" section_title="Terms" span_start="0" span_end="200">
By autonomy here I mean self-governance of reasons, not mere independence from others.
</passage>

label.text: Autonomy in this passage means self-governance of reasons rather than mere independence from others.

input:
<passage id="p0001" order="1" role="thesis" section_title="A" span_start="0" span_end="80">
Epistemic justification is fundamentally a normative status of belief.
</passage>

<passage id="p0002" order="2" role="premise" section_title="B" span_start="81" span_end="200">
Normative statuses answer to reasons, not merely causes.
</passage>

<passage id="p0003" order="3" role="response" section_title="C" span_start="201" span_end="320">
So causal reliability alone cannot exhaust justification without importing normativity.
</passage>

label.text: Epistemic justification is fundamentally a normative status of belief.
